page: Vultr voc-m-2c-16gb-400s-amd is an instance within the Optimized Cloud Compute family, featuring two shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor running at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The hardware configuration includes 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Storage is provided via a bundled 400 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No dedicated GPU hardware is present on this server type.

Benchmark data reveals a divergence between single-core and multi-core performance. The AMD EPYC-Rome processor achieves a strong single-core stress-ng CPU score of 2934.07, placing it in the top 25% of tested hardware. However, due to the shared CPU allocation and two-core limit, multi-core CPU performance is weak, scoring 5824.37 in stress-ng and 5470.65 in PassMark. Memory bandwidth tests show average performance for large block reads at 74.28 GB/sec and writes at 53.91 GB/sec, though small block cached reads are weak at 296.86 GB/sec.

The resource profile offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio and substantial NVMe storage density relative to its compute class, making it qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads that do not require sustained multi-core processing. Tradeoffs include poor static web serving throughput of 2.26 GB/sec and weak database operations scoring 1120.5 in PassMark. Consequently, this instance is suited for lightweight memory-optimized tasks, basic caching layers, or small-scale text generation using smaller language models, while being less optimal for high-concurrency web serving or intensive multi-threaded database applications.
description: The Vultr voc-m-2c-16gb-400s-amd is an x86_64 virtual server from the Optimized Cloud Compute family, configured with 2 shared AMD EPYC-Rome vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz, 16.0 GB of RAM, and a 400 GB NVMe SSD. While single-core CPU performance is strong, registering 2934.07 in stress-ng tests, the shared CPU allocation leads to weaker multi-core and database performance. Large block memory bandwidth remains average, with read speeds of 74.28 GB/sec and write speeds of 53.91 GB/sec. This server type provides high memory and storage density relative to its compute capacity, making it a qualitatively cost-effective option for memory-intensive workloads with low CPU concurrency. It is suited for lightweight caching, basic data processing, and small-scale language model inference, though it exhibits poor throughput for static web serving and heavy database operations.
og_description: Vultr voc-m-2c-16gb-400s-amd is an x86_64 server with 2 shared AMD EPYC vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, and 400 GB NVMe SSD, offering strong single-core speed but limited multi-core performance.
meta_description: Vultr voc-m-2c-16gb-400s-amd is an x86_64 server with 2 shared AMD EPYC vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, and 400 GB NVMe SSD for memory-focused workloads.
tagline: An AMD-powered virtual server combining high memory density and NVMe storage for lightweight, memory-intensive workloads.
bullet_points: AMD EPYC-Rome architecture with 2 shared vCPUs and 16.0 GB RAM
Bundled 400 GB NVMe SSD local storage
Strong single-core CPU performance offset by weak multi-core scaling
Average large-block memory read and write bandwidth
Best suited for lightweight caching, small-scale text generation, and memory-bound tasks
